abstract: Efficient C–C bond cleavage and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are fundamental challenges in developing high-performance direct alcohol fuel cells for renewable energy applications. In this work, we report the synthesis of core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along ultrathin (2.3 nm), stepped Au NW templates. This unique architecture serves as a robust catalyst for the effective oxidation of alcohols. We found that the catalytic performance is highly sensitive to the Au/Pt ratio; specifically, the Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—exhibit exceptional activity for ethanol electro-oxidation, achieving an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgₚₜ (32.5 A/mgₚₜ₊ₐᵤ). Beyond ethanol, these NWs are highly effective at catalyzing the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IRRAS), we characterized the catalyst’s structural evolution and identified key reaction intermediates. These measurements provide direct evidence that the synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and the stepped Au NW core is essential for facilitating the reaction. Furthermore, DFT calculations confirm that this under-coordinated Pt–Au surface significantly lowers the energy barrier for C–C bond cleavage. Our findings establish a general design strategy for core/shell catalysts with stepped surfaces, offering a pathway toward more efficient electro-oxidation reactions and the advancement of sustainable fuel cell technologies.
